techniques.evidence_quote: browsing shodan ... looking for exposed jenkins servers
browsing shodan (or well, technically zoomeye, chinese shodan)
an exposed jenkins server belonging to CommuteAir
we do have access to build workspaces ... we get to see the repositories that were built for each one of the ~70 build jobs
numerous s3 buckets, dozens of dynamodb tables
i now seemingly have access to pretty much their entire aws infrastructure via aws-cli
a file called application-prod.properties ... hardcoded credentials there would allow me access
User credentials to more than 40 Amazon S3 buckets and servers run by CommuteAir were also exposed
i have ended up finding AWS credentials. i now seemingly have access to pretty much their entire aws infrastructure via aws-cli
grabbing sample documents from various s3 buckets
three csv files, employee_information.csv, NOFLY.CSV and SELECTEE.CSV. all commited to the repository in july 2022
for the first time actually take a peek at the test data in the repository
the data is available for access (upon request) via DDoSecrets
available for access (upon request) via DDoSecrets

attack_chain.description: Researcher browsed Shodan/ZoomEye searching for exposed Jenkins servers, eventually locating CommuteAir's Jenkins instance.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: browsing shodan ... looking for exposed jenkins servers

attack_chain.description: Accessed the publicly exposed Jenkins server belonging to CommuteAir without authentication to reach build workspaces.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: an exposed jenkins server belonging to CommuteAir ... we do have access to build workspaces

attack_chain.description: Enumerated ~70 build job workspaces and repositories visible in Jenkins, including Spring Boot projects with resources directories.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: we get to see the repositories that were built for each one of the ~70 build jobs

evidence_chunks.quote: we get to see the repositories that were built for each one of the ~70 build jobs
we get to see the repositories that were built for each one of the ~70 build jobs

attack_chain.description: Found hardcoded credentials in application-prod.properties configuration files and other config files across multiple projects, including SFTP credentials for a Navtech/Navblue server and AWS credentials.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a file called application-prod.properties ... hardcoded credentials there would allow me access

attack_chain.description: Accessed the noflycomparison and noflycomparisonv2 project repositories and discovered test CSV data including NOFLY.CSV, SELECTEE.CSV, and employee_information.csv committed in July 2022.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: three csv files, employee_information.csv, NOFLY.CSV and SELECTEE.CSV. all commited to the repository in july 2022

attack_chain.description: Used harvested AWS credentials with aws-cli to access CommuteAir's AWS infrastructure.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: i have ended up finding AWS credentials. i now seemingly have access to pretty much their entire aws infrastructure via aws-cli

attack_chain.description: Enumerated CommuteAir's AWS environment, identifying numerous S3 buckets, dozens of DynamoDB tables, and servers.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: numerous s3 buckets, dozens of dynamodb tables, as well as various servers

evidence_chunks.quote: numerous s3 buckets, dozens of dynamodb tables, as well as various servers
numerous s3 buckets, dozens of dynamodb tables, as well as various servers

attack_chain.description: Downloaded sample documents from S3 buckets and dumped DynamoDB tables, obtaining crew PII, passport numbers, trip sheets, flight plans, and maintenance data.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: grabbing sample documents from various s3 buckets, going through flight plans and dumping some dynamodb tables

evidence_chunks.quote: grabbing sample documents from various s3 buckets, going through flight plans and dumping some dynamodb tables
grabbing sample documents from various s3 buckets, going through flight plans and dumping some dynamodb tables

attack_chain.description: Exfiltrated data and later made the 2019 No Fly List available through DDoSecrets for journalists and researchers.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the data is available for access (upon request) via DDoSecrets

initial_access_vector: Publicly exposed Jenkins server discovered via Shodan/ZoomEye, accessible without admin authentication but exposing build workspaces and repositories containing hardcoded credentials.
persistence_mechanism: None established; researcher relied on harvested long-lived AWS credentials from configuration files.
privilege_escalation: Harvested hardcoded AWS credentials from Jenkins build workspace config files, granting broad access across CommuteAir's AWS infrastructure.
